title: Cat Weight Loss Despite Increased Appetite: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat losing weight despite a ravenous appetite? Learn about hyperthyroidism, diabetes, and when to seek veterinary care for your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: A cat losing weight despite an increased appetite often indicates a metabolic or malabsorptive condition that requires a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours.

What does it mean if my cat is losing weight even though they are eating more than usual?

It can be deeply concerning to watch your cat finish every meal with gusto yet continue to look thinner. In the veterinary world, this is often called polyphagia with weight loss. This happens when a cat's body is either unable to absorb nutrients properly or is burning through energy at an abnormally high metabolic rate. Common causes include Hyperthyroidism (common in older cats), Diabetes Mellitus, and intestinal parasites or malabsorption issues like Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD).

Is it a veterinary emergency if my cat is losing weight while having a ravenous appetite?

  • The urgency level for weight loss with a ravenous appetite is Medium.
  • While it typically does not require an emergency room visit, seek immediate care if your cat is also vomiting, lethargic, or collapsing.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours to prevent metabolic crises or organ strain.

How can providing photos and videos of my cat help the veterinarian with triage?

  • Body Condition Assessment: Take a photo from directly above while your cat is standing to help the vet evaluate their "Body Condition Score" and muscle wasting.
  • Digestive Health: Provide a photo of their stool to assist in diagnosing potential malabsorption.
  • Behavioral Evidence: Record a video of the ravenous behavior to help the vet distinguish between true hunger and behavioral changes.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Weight loss despite increased appetite (polyphagia) in cats can be attributed to several underlying conditions. Hyperthyroidism is a common cause, often accompanied by hyperexcitability, polydipsia, polyuria, and a palpable enlarged thyroid gland; gastrointestinal signs such as vomiting and diarrhea may also be present. Malabsorption syndromes should also be considered, which typically manifest as chronic diarrhea, altered appetite (anorexia or polyphagia), and may be associated with coprophagia or pica. Systemic diseases may also cause weight loss, although they typically cause anorexia instead of polyphagia.

Chapter: Endocrinology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 559)

faqs.question: Could my cat just have worms?
faqs.answer: Yes, intestinal parasites can consume the nutrients your cat eats, leading to weight loss. However, in adult and senior cats, systemic issues like thyroid disease or diabetes are more frequent causes.

faqs.question: What tests will the vet perform?
faqs.answer: Typically, a vet will start with a physical exam and a 'senior' blood panel, which includes checking thyroid (T4) levels, blood glucose, and organ function, along with a fecal exam.

faqs.question: Is this behavior normal for an aging cat?
faqs.answer: No. While some muscle loss occurs with age, a ravenous appetite combined with weight loss is always a sign of an underlying medical condition and should not be dismissed as 'just getting old'.
